evidence: We also use and are investing in AI tools for our internal business operations, including to generate code, develop products and services, and increase operational efficiency.
Although we continue to invest in AI, those investments may be substantial, and there can be no assurance that our investments will be beneficial to our business.
Continuing to responsibly incorporate AI, machine learning, and automation into our products and services and associated business processes, such as customer support and software development

Several of these point-of-care analyzers, including the Catalyst One, ProCyte One, SediVue Dx, and IDEXX inVue Dx Cellular Analyzer, utilize proprietary AI capabilities in their image capture systems.
VetConnect PLUS enables greater medical insight through IDEXX DecisionIQ, an analytical tool that utilizes proprietary technology, including AI, to aid practitioners in making medical diagnoses.
The IDEXX ImageVue DR50 Plus, which launched in North America in January 2026, combines high definition, AI-powered imaging with a lower dose of radiation.
AI algorithms and models may be flawed. Datasets may be insufficient or contain information that is non-representative, infringing, or otherwise subject to legal challenge.

Our use of AI may also lead to novel cybersecurity or privacy risks... Existing and potential government regulation related to AI use may also foreclose certain areas of AI use, cause us to modify how we use AI.
The use of AI and other emerging technologies by threat actors is growing, enabling more sophisticated and effective cybersecurity attacks and phishing and social engineering schemes.

limitations: No quantified dollar amounts or headcount figures tied specifically to AI investment were found in the excerpts; investment intensity scored 3 (named programs, no quantified scale).
AI narrative concreteness scored 4 (deployed use cases with operating detail) rather than 5 because no quantified business results (%, $, time saved, volume) were disclosed in the excerpts.

Risk disclosure scored 4 (company-specific risk scenarios with mechanisms) rather than 5 because no quantified risk figures or named formal AI governance oversight bodies were cited in the excerpts; governance processes are referenced only generically ('implementing strong governance processes').
No AI-attributable capex or R&D dollar figures appeared in the excerpts; capex_mentions list is empty.
